patterns.name: Similar Word Mix-Up
patterns.what: Tends to pick a vocabulary word that has the right general idea but doesn't quite fit the specific situation.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Chose 'prevented' instead of 'impeded' when describing how a lack of confidence slows down an ability.
Picked 'intensive' instead of 'overwhelming' to describe a very strong smell.
Selected 'bustle' instead of 'flurry' when describing a sudden burst of activity.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When two words seem to mean the same thing, look closely at the words around the blank.
- **Read the whole sentence** to see if the word needs to fit a specific tone or pair with a specific preposition.
- **Try plugging in both options** to see which one sounds more natural in that exact context.

patterns.name: Phrasal Verb Mix-Up
patterns.what: Sometimes chooses the wrong ending word for a phrasal verb, which completely changes the meaning of the action.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Picked 'fell apart' instead of 'fell out' to describe friends having a misunderstanding.
Chose 'give away' instead of 'give off' for flowers producing a sweet fragrance.

patterns.strategic_advice:
Phrasal verbs are tricky because changing one small word changes the whole meaning!
- **Memorise common pairs** like 'fall out' (argue) vs 'fall apart' (break into pieces).
- **Use context clues** in the sentence to figure out what action is actually happening before picking the ending word.

patterns.name: Preposition Mix-Up
patterns.what: Occasionally selects the wrong preposition to follow a specific verb or adjective.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Chose 'over' instead of 'on' after the word 'congratulated'.
Picked 'from' instead of 'to' when using the word 'similar'.

patterns.strategic_advice:
Certain words are best friends and always stick together with specific prepositions.
- **Learn them as a pair**: When you learn a new word, learn its preposition partner too (e.g., 'similar to', 'confide in').
- **Read aloud**: Sometimes your ears can catch the right pair if you read the sentence softly to yourself.

patterns.name: I vs Me Mix-Up
patterns.what: Sometimes confuses when to use 'I' and when to use 'me' when there are two people mentioned in the sentence.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Chose 'I' instead of 'me' when the teacher was asking Sally and Mahdi12 to help.
Made the same slip again on a similar question, picking 'I' instead of 'me' as the receiver of the action.

patterns.strategic_advice:
There is a very simple trick to always get this right!
- **Hide the other person**: Cover up 'Sally and' with your finger and read the sentence again.
- **Listen to how it sounds**: 'My teacher has asked I' sounds wrong, but 'My teacher has asked **me**' sounds perfect!
